mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive Judaism addresses biblical era (A.1), Talmudic foundations (A.3), medieval (A.4), modern (A.5), contemporary (A.6). Torah and Talmud relate to biblical literature (B.1). Practices address C.2. Orthodox (C.3.a), Reform (C.3.b), Kabbalah (B.5). God (C.1.a) and covenant (C.1.b) are central.
The Hebrew Bible relates to biblical era (A.1) and TaNaKh (B.1.b).
Literary analysis of the Hebrew Bible addresses TaNaKh (B.1.b).
The Bible relates to biblical literature (B.1) and exegesis (B.4).
Dead Sea Scrolls relate to the Hellenistic era (A.2) and Qumran literature (B.2).
Jewish history addresses biblical (A.1), medieval (A.4), and modern (A.5) eras.
Modern Jewish literature relates to modern (A.5) and contemporary (A.6) Judaism.
Kabbalah addresses mystical writings (B.5) and Hasidism (C.3.d).
Maimonides relates to medieval Judaism (A.4) and philosophical writings (B.6).
Medieval philosophy relates to medieval era (A.4) and Jewish philosophy (B.6).
Biblical archaeology relates to the biblical era (A.1).
Zionism relates to modern Judaism (A.5) and sacred places (C.2.d).
Examines the Israeli-Palestinian conflict in relation to Jewish religious claims to Jerusalem and the land of Israel, relevant to 826.C.2.d.
Antisemitism addresses medieval (A.4) and modern (A.5) Jewish history.
The ghetto relates to medieval (A.4) and modern (A.5) eras.
The Holocaust relates to modern Judaism (A.5).
Diaspora relates to modern (A.5) and contemporary (A.6) Judaism.
Conceptions of God relate to Jewish doctrines concerning God (C.1.a).
Judaism as Abrahamic tradition relates to history (A), God (C.1.a), and Covenant (C.1.b).
Paul's relationship to Jewish law relates to the Hellenistic era (A.2).
The Enlightenment provides context for modern Judaism (A.5).
Hermeneutics relates to Judaic exegesis (B.4).
Ritual relates to Jewish practices (C.2).
The Crusades' impact on Jews relates to the medieval era (A.4).
American Judaism relates to contemporary demographics (A.6).
Covers Jewish philosophers including Solomon Ibn Gabirol within the Islamic philosophical tradition, directly relevant to the medieval era of rabbinic Judaism and Jewish philosophical writings.
Addresses the relationship between Christian imagery and Jewish visual traditions, directly relevant to the anti-iconic principle and uses of the visual arts in ceremony and ritual within Judaism.

Covers theology across major world religions including Judaism, examining concepts of God, salvation, the interpretation of sacred texts, and hermeneutics, directly relevant to basic beliefs and doctrines and Judaic exegesis.

Covers non-Western and folk musical traditions including the relationship between music and cultural identity, relevant to Jewish liturgical modes and the influence of folk traditions on Jewish vocal and instrumental music.

Covers Hebrew literature as part of the multilingual literary traditions of Spain including medieval Iberian literature, relevant to the medieval era of rabbinic Judaism and Jewish literary traditions in the European and Islamic phases.
